$comment: Cross-language conformance vectors for stable_anchor_id. ONE source of truth consumed by BOTH core/view/src/anchor_strategy.cpp (via test/test_design_import_anchors.cpp) and packages/pulp-import-ir/src/anchors.ts (via packages/pulp-import-ir/test/anchors.test.ts). Both headers claim the two implementations mirror each other exactly. For ASCII they do. For non-ASCII text they DO NOT, and this file is the evidence: `cpp_anchor` and `ts_anchor` are both recorded, and every vector where they differ carries `known_divergence`. A pulp-tweaks.json keyed by one pipeline silently fails to match in the other for those vectors — the TS applyTweaks records orphaned_tweaks, the C++ side just does not apply the edit, and the user sees their edit stop sticking with no error.

$divergence_axes.utf8-vs-utf16: The FNV-1a fold consumes different units. C++ fnv1a_base36() iterates `char` and folds each UTF-8 BYTE; the TS hashCodeAsString() uses str.charCodeAt(i) and folds each UTF-16 CODE UNIT. 'o with diaeresis' is one 0x00F6 code unit in TS but two bytes (0xC3 0xB6) in C++, so the two hashes diverge for any text outside ASCII. Proven in isolation by nonascii-umlaut-lowercase, which involves no case mapping and no whitespace.
$divergence_axes.ascii-only-case-folding: TS normalizes with String.prototype.toLowerCase(), which is full-Unicode. C++ normalize_text() lowercases only A-Z by construction. Proven by the pair nonascii-umlaut-{lower,upper}case: TS produces the SAME anchor for both (it folds the capital), C++ produces two DIFFERENT anchors.
$divergence_axes.ascii-only-whitespace-class: TS normalizes with /\s+/, whose class includes U+00A0 NBSP, U+000B, U+000C, U+2028/9 and the Unicode space separators. C++ normalize_text() recognizes only space, tab, CR and LF. Proven by the pair ascii-plain-space-control / nonascii-nbsp-whitespace: TS collapses the NBSP and lands on the plain-space anchor, C++ keeps the raw NBSP bytes and lands elsewhere.
$divergence_axes.adapter-missing-id-softfail-vs-throw: NOT a hashing difference, and NOT covered by the recorded vectors — they only exercise the adapter happy path (source_node_id present). When source_node_id/adapter is EMPTY, C++ anchor_strategy.cpp:204-224 soft-falls back to content-hash and silently returns an anchor, while TS anchors.ts:101-109 THROWS. So the two lanes disagree on whether a malformed adapter node is an error at all: C++ emits a content-hash anchor that TS would never produce, and a tweak keyed to it can never match. The C++ comment records the soft-fail as deliberate (a missing anchor breaks the inspector silently, a recovered one lets the pipeline finish) — it is a known design divergence, not a bug, but it must be settled alongside the encoding choice because it changes which anchors exist, not just what they hash to.

$expected_values_generated_from: core/view/src/anchor_strategy.cpp — the C++ implementation is the RECORDED REFERENCE here purely because it is the pipeline the shipped native importer runs. That is a description of today's behavior, NOT a ruling that UTF-8 bytes are the canonical encoding. Choosing the canonical side is a human decision: either choice re-keys every existing pulp-tweaks.json that contains non-ASCII text, so the fix is a deliberate, migration-bearing change and is intentionally out of scope for this harness. This file exists to make the drift provable and impossible to merge over silently.
$regenerate: These vectors are asserted, not derived — regenerate only when you INTEND to change anchor output. Recompute cpp_anchor by calling pulp::view::compute_anchor_id() over each `input`, and ts_anchor by calling generateAnchorId() from @pulp/import-ir, then re-run both suites.

vectors.name: ascii-uppercase-folds
vectors.note: ASCII control — both sides lowercase A-Z identically.

vectors.name: ascii-whitespace-collapses
vectors.note: ASCII control — runs of space/tab/newline collapse to one space, then trim.

vectors.name: ascii-with-role-and-sig-index
vectors.note: ASCII control — exercises role + depth + duplicate-sibling discriminator.

vectors.name: nonascii-umlaut-lowercase
vectors.note: Already-lowercase o-umlaut. Isolates the FNV input-encoding axis from the case-folding axis: no case mapping is involved, so any divergence here is purely UTF-8 bytes vs UTF-16 code units.

vectors.name: nonascii-umlaut-uppercase
vectors.note: Uppercase O-umlaut. Compounds two axes: TS toLowerCase() folds it to o-umlaut, C++ ASCII-only fold leaves it uppercase, and then the two hash different encodings.

vectors.name: nonascii-degree-sign
vectors.note: Degree sign — extremely common in audio-plugin knob labels.

vectors.name: nonascii-trademark
vectors.note: Trademark sign — 3 UTF-8 bytes, 1 UTF-16 code unit.

vectors.name: nonascii-cjk
vectors.note: CJK (Japanese 'volume') — 3 UTF-8 bytes per char, 1 UTF-16 code unit per char.

vectors.name: nonascii-emoji-surrogate-pair
vectors.note: Control-knob emoji U+1F39B — 4 UTF-8 bytes, and a UTF-16 SURROGATE PAIR (2 code units). Worst case for the encoding split.

vectors.name: ascii-plain-space-control
vectors.note: Regular ASCII space between two letters. Paired with nonascii-nbsp-whitespace to prove the whitespace-class axis: TS collapses NBSP to a plain space (so its NBSP anchor equals THIS anchor), while C++ keeps the raw NBSP bytes (so its NBSP anchor does not).

vectors.name: nonascii-nbsp-whitespace
vectors.note: Non-breaking space between two ASCII letters. Third divergence axis: the TS /\s+/ regex treats NBSP as whitespace and collapses it to a plain space; the C++ normalizer only recognizes space/tab/CR/LF and passes NBSP through as two literal bytes.

vectors.name: nonascii-tag-name
vectors.note: Non-ASCII in the TAG rather than the text — tags are never normalized, so this isolates the encoding axis in a second field.

vectors.name: path-nested
vectors.note: Path strategy is pure string concatenation — no hashing, so encoding cannot matter. Serves as a cross-language control proving the harness is not simply reporting 'everything diverges'.

vectors.name: path-nonascii-tag
vectors.note: Non-ASCII tag under the path strategy — MUST agree across languages precisely because no hash is involved.

vectors.name: adapter-nonascii-source-id
vectors.note: Non-ASCII native id under the adapter strategy — control; must agree.
